text: 1. To obtain an austenitic structure and good physicochemical properties Fe—Mn—Al alloys should contain 8-10% Al, 25-30% Mn, and \~1% C. An increase of the aluminum content and decrease of the manganese and carbon content results in α-phase in the structure, which impairs the magnetic and plastic characteristics of the alloys.

2. After quenching from 1150°C and aging 16 h at 550°C the alloys of the optimal composition have the following mechanical properties: $\sigma_{\mathrm{b}} = 90 - 95\mathrm{kg / mm}^2$ ; $\sigma_{0,2} = 80 - 85\mathrm{kg / mm}^2$ ; $\delta = 10 - 20\%$ ; $\psi = 15 - 30\%$ .
3. Alloys of the optimal composition have a density of 6.7-6.8 g/cm $^{3}$ and a substantially higher strength than the widely used Cr-Ni steel 10Kh2N24 or the Mn-Al steel 45G17Yu3.
4. Alloys with 8-10% Al have the same oxidation resistance as stainless steel 1Kh17N2. # LITERATURE CITED
